g.text: RGA (Replicated Growable Array), Y.js, Automerge u2014 sequence types

text.tspan: Per-replica counter. G-counter: each replica only increments its OWN slot.
state = {A: 5, B: 3, C: 7}
value = sum = 15
merge(state1, state2) = element-wise max
PN-counter: a pair of G-counters (one for inc, one for dec).
USED FOR: like counts, view counts, vote counts across replicas
G-Set: add-only set; merge = union. Trivially CRDT.
2P-Set: two G-sets (added, removed); element 'in set' if added and not removed.
OR-Set: tags each add with a unique ID; remove only removes adds you saw.
u2192 OR-Set solves the 'add-after-remove' race that 2P-Set can't.
USED FOR: tag lists, collaborative sets, shared inventories
LWW-Register: timestamp each write; on merge, keep highest-timestamp value.
u2192 simple but DROPS concurrent writes; needs synced clocks (HLC helps)
MV-Register: returns ALL concurrent values; app resolves conflict.
u2192 e.g., Riak/Dynamo show 'siblings' for the app to resolve
USED FOR: single-value fields where concurrent writes are rare or app handles them
ORDERED sequences that support concurrent inserts/deletes at any position.
The hard CRDT case u2014 text editing, collaborative documents.
Uses tombstones, intent-preserving operation transforms, unique IDs per char.
USED FOR: collaborative text editors (Google Docs uses OT, not CRDT;
but Figma, Apple Notes, Notion's offline mode, Yjs all use CRDTs)
Maps where keys can be added/removed, values are themselves CRDTs.
Nested JSON CRDTs (Automerge, Yjs) compose all the above into arbitrary docs.
USED FOR: shared JSON documents, real-time collaborative apps
